URGENT: Your Apple account has verify your identity within 24 hours. Click the secure link below and confirm your password and card details now: 235.145.237.121/login

evidence.raw_content_sample: URGENT: Your Apple account has verify your identity within 24 hours. Click the secure link below and confirm your password and card details now: 235.145.237.121/login

confidence_score.basis: final_risk_probability=0.91 weighted with cross-signal agreement=0.40 (anomaly_score and phishing_probability diverge)

playbook.root_cause: [MOCK LLM OUTPUT — SOC_LLM_MOCK_MODE=1, NOT A REAL MODEL RESPONSE] (would have sent a 946-char prompt to claude-sonnet-4-6)
immediate_actions: Isolate/quarantine traffic from source IP 60.48.6.241 pending investigation.
Preserve logs and relevant packet captures for the affected window before any remediation that could overwrite them.
Quarantine the reported message across all mailboxes (search-and-purge by sender/subject/hash).
Block the sender domain and any embedded URLs at the email gateway.
Notify the targeted user(s) and any recipients who interacted with the message.
Force a password reset + re-authentication (all sessions) for any user who submitted credentials.
linux_commands:
# Block the source IP at the host firewall
sudo iptables -A INPUT -s 60.48.6.241 -j DROP

# Confirm the block is in place
sudo iptables -L INPUT -v -n | grep 60.48.6.241

# Capture current network connections for evidence
ss -tunap > /tmp/soc_evidence_connections_$(date +%s).txt
windows_commands:
# Block the source IP via Windows Firewall
New-NetFirewallRule -DisplayName "SOC-Block-60.48.6.241" -Direction Inbound -RemoteAddress 60.48.6.241 -Action Block

# Capture current network connections for evidence
Get-NetTCPConnection | Export-Csv -Path C:\soc_evidence\connections.csv
firewall_rules:
# Generic (iptables) — block
iptables -A INPUT -s 60.48.6.241 -j DROP

# Generic (nftables) — block
nft add rule inet filter input ip saddr 60.48.6.241 drop

# Cisco ASA — block
access-list SOC_BLOCK deny ip host 60.48.6.241 any
# pfSense/OPNsense (alias-based) — add 60.48.6.241 to a 'SOC_Blocklist' alias and apply the existing deny rule referencing it.
playbook.yara_rule:
rule SOC_Phishing_60_48_6_241
{
    meta:
        description = "Auto-generated by SOC Assistant (Module 4) from a flagged high-risk event"
        source_ip = "60.48.6.241"
        generated = "template-driven, not LLM-authored — review before deployment"

    strings:
        $ioc_0 = "60.48.6.241" nocase

    condition:
        any of ($ioc_*)
}
playbook.snort_rule: alert tcp 60.48.6.241 any -> $HOME_NET any (msg:"SOC: Phishing (Email) from 60.48.6.241"; content:"60.48.6.241"; nocase; sid:1000001; rev:1; classtype:trojan-activity;)
playbook.sigma_rule:
title: SOC Auto-Generated Detection — Phishing (Email)
status: experimental
description: Generated by SOC Assistant (Module 4) from a flagged high-risk event. Review before deployment.
logsource:
    category: network
    product: generic
detection:
    selection:
        src_ip: '60.48.6.241'
        payload|contains:
        - '60.48.6.241'
    condition: selection
level: high
tags:
    - soc.autogenerated

cve_suggestions.disclaimer: Illustrative CVEs commonly associated with this attack category — NOT a confirmed match for this specific event. This platform has no software/version inventory for the affected host; confirm applicability against actual asset data before acting on any of these.

candidates.name: Microsoft Outlook Elevation of Privilege (NTLM hash theft via crafted email)
candidates.note: Relevant if the organization runs Outlook for Windows and the email contains reminder/UNC-path artifacts.

recovery_steps: Confirm the source of compromise is fully contained (no active sessions/connections remain).
Restore affected accounts/services from known-good state; rotate any credentials that may have been exposed.
Re-scan previously affected hosts for persistence mechanisms before returning them to production.
Validate that the deployed firewall/detection rules from this playbook are active and correctly matching.
Monitor the source IP/domain/account for 14 days for renewed activity before closing the incident.
post_incident_checklist: [ ] Root cause confirmed and documented (not just inferred from SHAP evidence)
[ ] All immediate actions completed and verified
[ ] Detection rules (YARA/Snort/Sigma) deployed to production and validated against a known-good test case
[ ] Affected users/systems notified per incident communication policy
[ ] Playbook and evidence archived for compliance / audit
[ ] Lessons-learned review scheduled with the team
[ ] Any newly-identified detection gaps fed back into Module 1/Module 2 training data

immediate_actions: Isolate/quarantine traffic from source IP 139.196.115.201 pending investigation.
Preserve logs and relevant packet captures for the affected window before any remediation that could overwrite them.
Block the source IP at the WAF; add a temporary rate-limit rule on the targeted endpoint.
Review application logs for successful responses (200/302) to the same payload pattern from other sources.
Snapshot the affected service/container for forensic review before any restart.
linux_commands:
# Block the source IP at the host firewall
sudo iptables -A INPUT -s 139.196.115.201 -j DROP

# Confirm the block is in place
sudo iptables -L INPUT -v -n | grep 139.196.115.201

# Capture current network connections for evidence
ss -tunap > /tmp/soc_evidence_connections_$(date +%s).txt

# Search web server logs for the same payload pattern
sudo grep -E "(UNION|OR '1'='1'|<script>)" /var/log/nginx/access.log | grep '139.196.115.201'

# Snapshot the container/service for forensic review
docker commit <container_id> forensic_snapshot_$(date +%s)
windows_commands:
# Block the source IP via Windows Firewall
New-NetFirewallRule -DisplayName "SOC-Block-139.196.115.201" -Direction Inbound -RemoteAddress 139.196.115.201 -Action Block

# Capture current network connections for evidence
Get-NetTCPConnection | Export-Csv -Path C:\soc_evidence\connections.csv

# Search IIS logs for the same payload pattern
Select-String -Path C:\inetpub\logs\LogFiles\*.log -Pattern '139.196.115.201'
firewall_rules:
# Generic (iptables) — block
iptables -A INPUT -s 139.196.115.201 -j DROP

# Generic (nftables) — block
nft add rule inet filter input ip saddr 139.196.115.201 drop

# Cisco ASA — block
access-list SOC_BLOCK deny ip host 139.196.115.201 any
# pfSense/OPNsense (alias-based) — add 139.196.115.201 to a 'SOC_Blocklist' alias and apply the existing deny rule referencing it.

rule SOC_Web_API_139_196_115_201

        description = "Auto-generated by SOC Assistant (Module 4) from a flagged high-risk event"
        source_ip = "139.196.115.201"
        generated = "template-driven, not LLM-authored — review before deployment"

        $ioc_1 = "139.196.115.201" nocase

immediate_actions: Isolate/quarantine traffic from source IP 139.196.115.201 pending investigation.
Preserve logs and relevant packet captures for the affected window before any remediation that could overwrite them.
Lock or force-reset the targeted account(s); invalidate active sessions/tokens.
Enforce MFA re-enrollment for the affected account.
Block the source IP at the perimeter firewall / WAF.
Review authentication logs for the account over the preceding 30 days for earlier compromise indicators.
linux_commands:
# Block the source IP at the host firewall
sudo iptables -A INPUT -s 139.196.115.201 -j DROP

# Confirm the block is in place
sudo iptables -L INPUT -v -n | grep 139.196.115.201

# Capture current network connections for evidence
ss -tunap > /tmp/soc_evidence_connections_$(date +%s).txt

# Review recent auth failures from this source
sudo grep '139.196.115.201' /var/log/auth.log | tail -100

# Force logout of all active sessions for a compromised local user (replace USERNAME)
sudo pkill -KILL -u USERNAME
windows_commands:
# Block the source IP via Windows Firewall
New-NetFirewallRule -DisplayName "SOC-Block-139.196.115.201" -Direction Inbound -RemoteAddress 139.196.115.201 -Action Block

# Capture current network connections for evidence
Get-NetTCPConnection | Export-Csv -Path C:\soc_evidence\connections.csv

# Review recent failed logons from this source (Event ID 4625)
Get-WinEvent -FilterHashtable @{LogName='Security'; Id=4625} | Where-Object {$_.Message -match '139.196.115.201'}

# Force sign-out and disable a compromised account (replace USERNAME)
Disable-ADAccount -Identity USERNAME
firewall_rules:
# Generic (iptables) — block
iptables -A INPUT -s 139.196.115.201 -j DROP

# Generic (nftables) — block
nft add rule inet filter input ip saddr 139.196.115.201 drop

# Cisco ASA — block
access-list SOC_BLOCK deny ip host 139.196.115.201 any
# pfSense/OPNsense (alias-based) — add 139.196.115.201 to a 'SOC_Blocklist' alias and apply the existing deny rule referencing it.

Sofia, your table for 5 is confirmed for 2:15pm.
endpoint=/api/v1/search method=POST param="{{7*7}}" requests_per_min=452 auth_header_present=True user_agent=curl/7.68 anomalous_payload_size=False

playbook.threat_summary: A critical-risk Web/API Exploitation attempt was detected originating from source IP 60.48.6.241, targeting the `/api/v1/search` endpoint with a POST request containing the payload parameter `"{{7*7}}"` via a curl user agent. The detection is evaluated with a high risk probability of 0.829, driven primarily by anomalous traffic features including a high burst score, failed connection rate, and an active volume of 452 requests per minute. This event is highly significant as it represents an active injection or abuse attempt on an authenticated API endpoint, posing a direct threat to the application's integrity.

evidence.raw_content_sample: endpoint=/api/v1/search method=POST param="{{7*7}}" requests_per_min=452 auth_header_present=True user_agent=curl/7.68 anomalous_payload_size=False

confidence_score.basis: final_risk_probability=0.83 weighted with cross-signal agreement=0.42 (anomaly_score and phishing_probability partially agree)

playbook.root_cause: The detection was primarily driven by high `burst_score` and `failed_connection_rate` signals, which, combined with a high request volume of 452 per minute, suggest an automated exploitation attempt from source IP 60.48.6.241. The presence of the `{{7*7}}` payload in a POST request to the `/api/v1/search` endpoint indicates a probable Server-Side Template Injection (SSTI) attack mechanism conducted via a `curl` script. Based on the model’s feature contributions and the `Web/API Exploitation` threat category, this is a probable inference of an injection-based attack rather than a confirmed forensic finding.

Preserve logs and relevant packet captures for the affected window before any remediation that could overwrite them.
Block the source IP at the WAF; add a temporary rate-limit rule on the targeted endpoint.
Review application logs for successful responses (200/302) to the same payload pattern from other sources.
Snapshot the affected service/container for forensic review before any restart.

playbook.executive_summary: Our security systems successfully detected a critical attempt to exploit our online search service (specifically the `/api/v1/search` system) originating from the external address 60.48.6.241. The attacker used rapid, automated requests in an attempt to inject unauthorized commands directly into our database. If successful, this type of manipulation could lead to system disruption, data exposure, or unauthorized access to our digital environment. The security team is actively reviewing the traffic from this source, verifying that our defenses blocked the malicious payloads, and monitoring our network to prevent further connection spikes.

immediate_actions: Isolate/quarantine traffic from source IP 222.174.254.167 pending investigation.
Preserve logs and relevant packet captures for the affected window before any remediation that could overwrite them.
Block the source IP at the WAF; add a temporary rate-limit rule on the targeted endpoint.
Review application logs for successful responses (200/302) to the same payload pattern from other sources.
Snapshot the affected service/container for forensic review before any restart.
linux_commands:
# Block the source IP at the host firewall
sudo iptables -A INPUT -s 222.174.254.167 -j DROP

# Confirm the block is in place
sudo iptables -L INPUT -v -n | grep 222.174.254.167

# Capture current network connections for evidence
ss -tunap > /tmp/soc_evidence_connections_$(date +%s).txt

# Search web server logs for the same payload pattern
sudo grep -E "(UNION|OR '1'='1'|<script>)" /var/log/nginx/access.log | grep '222.174.254.167'

# Snapshot the container/service for forensic review
docker commit <container_id> forensic_snapshot_$(date +%s)
windows_commands:
# Block the source IP via Windows Firewall
New-NetFirewallRule -DisplayName "SOC-Block-222.174.254.167" -Direction Inbound -RemoteAddress 222.174.254.167 -Action Block

# Capture current network connections for evidence
Get-NetTCPConnection | Export-Csv -Path C:\soc_evidence\connections.csv

# Search IIS logs for the same payload pattern
Select-String -Path C:\inetpub\logs\LogFiles\*.log -Pattern '222.174.254.167'
firewall_rules:
# Generic (iptables) — block
iptables -A INPUT -s 222.174.254.167 -j DROP

# Generic (nftables) — block
nft add rule inet filter input ip saddr 222.174.254.167 drop

# Cisco ASA — block
access-list SOC_BLOCK deny ip host 222.174.254.167 any
# pfSense/OPNsense (alias-based) — add 222.174.254.167 to a 'SOC_Blocklist' alias and apply the existing deny rule referencing it.

immediate_actions: Isolate/quarantine traffic from source IP 60.48.6.241 pending investigation.
Preserve logs and relevant packet captures for the affected window before any remediation that could overwrite them.
Lock or force-reset the targeted account(s); invalidate active sessions/tokens.
Enforce MFA re-enrollment for the affected account.
Block the source IP at the perimeter firewall / WAF.
Review authentication logs for the account over the preceding 30 days for earlier compromise indicators.
linux_commands:
# Block the source IP at the host firewall
sudo iptables -A INPUT -s 60.48.6.241 -j DROP

# Confirm the block is in place
sudo iptables -L INPUT -v -n | grep 60.48.6.241

# Capture current network connections for evidence
ss -tunap > /tmp/soc_evidence_connections_$(date +%s).txt

# Review recent auth failures from this source
sudo grep '60.48.6.241' /var/log/auth.log | tail -100

# Force logout of all active sessions for a compromised local user (replace USERNAME)
sudo pkill -KILL -u USERNAME
windows_commands:
# Block the source IP via Windows Firewall
New-NetFirewallRule -DisplayName "SOC-Block-60.48.6.241" -Direction Inbound -RemoteAddress 60.48.6.241 -Action Block

# Capture current network connections for evidence
Get-NetTCPConnection | Export-Csv -Path C:\soc_evidence\connections.csv

# Review recent failed logons from this source (Event ID 4625)
Get-WinEvent -FilterHashtable @{LogName='Security'; Id=4625} | Where-Object {$_.Message -match '60.48.6.241'}

# Force sign-out and disable a compromised account (replace USERNAME)
Disable-ADAccount -Identity USERNAME
firewall_rules:
# Generic (iptables) — block
iptables -A INPUT -s 60.48.6.241 -j DROP

# Generic (nftables) — block
nft add rule inet filter input ip saddr 60.48.6.241 drop

# Cisco ASA — block
access-list SOC_BLOCK deny ip host 60.48.6.241 any

cve_suggestions.disclaimer: Illustrative CVEs commonly associated with this attack category — NOT a confirmed match for this specific event. This platform has no software/version inventory for the affected host; confirm applicability against actual asset data before acting on any of these.

candidates.name: Microsoft Outlook Elevation of Privilege (NTLM hash theft via crafted email)
candidates.note: Relevant if the organization runs Outlook for Windows and the email contains reminder/UNC-path artifacts.

recovery_steps: Confirm the source of compromise is fully contained (no active sessions/connections remain).
Restore affected accounts/services from known-good state; rotate any credentials that may have been exposed.
Re-scan previously affected hosts for persistence mechanisms before returning them to production.
Validate that the deployed firewall/detection rules from this playbook are active and correctly matching.
Monitor the source IP/domain/account for 14 days for renewed activity before closing the incident.
post_incident_checklist: [ ] Root cause confirmed and documented (not just inferred from SHAP evidence)
[ ] All immediate actions completed and verified
[ ] Detection rules (YARA/Snort/Sigma) deployed to production and validated against a known-good test case
[ ] Affected users/systems notified per incident communication policy
[ ] Playbook and evidence archived for compliance / audit
[ ] Lessons-learned review scheduled with the team
[ ] Any newly-identified detection gaps fed back into Module 1/Module 2 training data

confidence_score.basis: final_risk_probability=0.74 weighted with cross-signal agreement=0.37 (anomaly_score and phishing_probability diverge)

playbook.root_cause: [MOCK LLM OUTPUT — SOC_LLM_MOCK_MODE=1, NOT A REAL MODEL RESPONSE] (would have sent a 816-char prompt to gemini-3.5-flash)
immediate_actions: Isolate/quarantine traffic from source IP 118.186.88.73 pending investigation.
Preserve logs and relevant packet captures for the affected window before any remediation that could overwrite them.
Block the URL/domain at the web proxy and DNS resolver.
Check proxy/DNS logs for any other users who resolved or visited the domain.
linux_commands:
# Block the source IP at the host firewall
sudo iptables -A INPUT -s 118.186.88.73 -j DROP

# Confirm the block is in place
sudo iptables -L INPUT -v -n | grep 118.186.88.73

# Capture current network connections for evidence
ss -tunap > /tmp/soc_evidence_connections_$(date +%s).txt
windows_commands:
# Block the source IP via Windows Firewall
New-NetFirewallRule -DisplayName "SOC-Block-118.186.88.73" -Direction Inbound -RemoteAddress 118.186.88.73 -Action Block

# Capture current network connections for evidence
Get-NetTCPConnection | Export-Csv -Path C:\soc_evidence\connections.csv
firewall_rules:
# Generic (iptables) — block
iptables -A INPUT -s 118.186.88.73 -j DROP

# Generic (nftables) — block
nft add rule inet filter input ip saddr 118.186.88.73 drop

# Cisco ASA — block
access-list SOC_BLOCK deny ip host 118.186.88.73 any
# pfSense/OPNsense (alias-based) — add 118.186.88.73 to a 'SOC_Blocklist' alias and apply the existing deny rule referencing it.
playbook.yara_rule:
rule SOC_Phishing_118_186_88_73
{
    meta:
        description = "Auto-generated by SOC Assistant (Module 4) from a flagged high-risk event"
        source_ip = "118.186.88.73"
        generated = "template-driven, not LLM-authored — review before deployment"

    strings:
        $ioc_0 = "https://www.chasebank-official.com/login" nocase
        $ioc_1 = "118.186.88.73" nocase

    condition:
        any of ($ioc_*)
}
playbook.snort_rule: alert tcp 118.186.88.73 any -> $HOME_NET any (msg:"SOC: Phishing (Malicious URL) from 118.186.88.73"; content:"https://www.chasebank-official.com/login"; nocase; content:"118.186.88.73"; nocase; sid:1000001; rev:1; classtype:trojan-activity;)
playbook.sigma_rule:
title: SOC Auto-Generated Detection — Phishing (Malicious URL)
status: experimental
description: Generated by SOC Assistant (Module 4) from a flagged high-risk event. Review before deployment.
logsource:
    category: network
    product: generic
detection:
    selection:
        src_ip: '118.186.88.73'
        payload|contains:
        - 'https://www.chasebank-official.com/login'
        - '118.186.88.73'
    condition: selection
level: high
tags:
    - soc.autogenerated
